did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP) neural network for automated melanoma detection. It employs Haar wavelet-based feature extraction from dermoscopic images and trains the MLP classifier using optimization algorithms such as Levenberg-Marquardt (LM), Scaled Conjugate Gradient (SCG), and Gradient Descent with Momentum (GDM) to distinguish between benign and malignant skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing an automated melanoma detection system using Haar wavelet-based feature extraction and MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP) neural networks. It details a specific methodology including data collection from a Kaggle dataset, system design, feature extraction, and classification using optimization algorithms (LM, SCG, GDM). The paper reports original experimental results, including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and confusion matrices, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Automated Melanoma Detection Using Haar Wavelet-Based Neural Networks
journal: 2024 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Electrical Engineering, Computer and Information Technology (ICEECIT)
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated melanoma detection system utilizing Haar wavelet-based feature extraction and MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP) neural networks to distinguish between benign and malignant skin lesions. The researchers collected 1,783 dermoscopic images from a Kaggle dataset, categorizing them into melanoma, nevus, and seborrheic keratosis classes. Haar wavelet transforms were employed to extract critical texture and edge features, which were then used to train MLP classifiers optimized by Levenberg-Marquardt (LM), Scaled Conjugate Gradient (SCG), and Gradient Descent with Momentum (GDM) algorithms. The evaluation focused on classification performance metrics, with the LM algorithm demonstrating superior results. The system achieved a testing accuracy of 87.3% with 20 hidden neurons, highlighting its potential to assist dermatologists in early and accurate melanoma diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant skin cancer class used in the experiment; 299 images from the Kaggle dataset.
Benign skin lesion class used in the experiment; 1,261 images from the Kaggle dataset.
Benign skin lesion class used in the experiment; 223 images from the Kaggle dataset.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (melanoma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes an automated melanoma detection system using Haar wavelet-based feature extraction and MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP) neural networks for classification. The model predicts discrete classes: melanoma (class 1), nevus (class 2), and seborrheic keratosis (class 3). The study evaluates classification performance using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and confusion matrices, with the Levenberg-Marquardt (LM) algorithm achieving a testing accuracy of 87.3%.

datasets_used.name: Skin Lesion Analysis Toward Melanoma Detection
datasets_used.description: An open-source dataset available on Kaggle (www.kaggle.com/datasets/wanderdust/skin-lesion-analysis-toward-melanoma-detection) containing dermoscopic images of skin cancer.

use.training: Used to train the MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP) neural network classifiers optimized by Levenberg-Marquardt (LM), Scaled Conjugate Gradient (SCG), and Gradient Descent with Momentum (GDM).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the classification performance (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) of the proposed system.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To summarize feature extraction results (e.g., Haar wavelet features) across different lesion classes and to report the average performance metrics (training, validation, testing accuracy) with variability across multiple runs for the MLP-SCG and MLP-GDM models.
To evaluate classification performance by analyzing true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives for the MLP-SCG and MLP-GDM models.
To assess the classifier's ability to distinguish between true positive rates and false positive rates for the MLP-SCG model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation) and classification metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, confusion matrices, ROC curves).

feature_extraction_results.description: Haar wavelet feature extraction results (Mean ± Standard Deviation) for 9 features across three classes.

MLP-LM (Levenberg-Marquardt).notes: The model achieved optimal performance at epoch 6. The best confusion matrix indicates training errors less than 10%, while testing and validation errors are more than 10%. The best ROC curve point is 0.8-0.9, categorized as good classification.

MLP-SCG (Scaled Conjugate Gradient).notes: The model converged at epoch 22. The best confusion matrix indicates errors during training, testing, and validation are more than 10%. The best ROC curve point is 0.8-0.9, categorized as good classification.

MLP-GDM (Gradient Descent with Momentum).notes: The best confusion matrix indicates errors during training, testing, and validation are more than 10%. The best ROC curve point is 0.8-0.9, categorized as good classification.

architecture_details.layers: Input layer, one or more hidden layers, output layer
architecture_details.best_configuration: 20 hidden neurons for LM and SCG; 15 hidden neurons for GDM
architecture_details.feature_input: 9 Haar wavelet-based features (texture and edge details)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: MLP with Scaled Conjugate Gradient (SCG) algorithm
MLP with Gradient Descent with Momentum (GDM) algorithm
preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of RGB dermoscopic images to grayscale to reduce complexity while preserving intensity information (edges and texture).
Resizing images from original resolution of 3520×2160 pixels to 600×600 pixels to standardize input data and reduce computational load.
Adaptive Histogram Equalization (adaptHistEq) to enhance image contrast and improve the distinguishability of lesion boundaries and textures.
conclusions: The Levenberg-Marquardt (LM) algorithm achieved the highest testing accuracy (87.3%) among the tested optimization methods when used with a MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP) and 20 hidden neurons.
Haar wavelet-based feature extraction effectively captures texture and edge details crucial for distinguishing between benign (nevus, seborrheic keratosis) and malignant (melanoma) skin lesions.

The proposed automated system demonstrates potential to assist dermatologists in clinical decision-making by providing more accurate and objective assessments of skin lesions compared to subjective visual examination.
Increasing the number of hidden neurons to 20 improved generalization and classification accuracy for the MLP-LM model, achieving optimal performance at epoch 6.
The MLP-SCG model with 20 hidden neurons showed robust generalization with similar validation and testing accuracies, converging effectively at epoch 22 without significant overfitting.

The MLP-GDM model with 15 hidden neurons showed signs of potential overfitting in some runs, with testing accuracy generally lower than training accuracy, though it achieved a competitive testing accuracy of 75.3% in its best run.
All three optimization algorithms (LM, SCG, GDM) categorized the system as having 'good classification' capability based on ROC curve points between 0.8 and 0.9.
The system effectively reduces false positives and negatives, contributing to early melanoma detection and potentially improving patient outcomes.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, single-source dataset (1,783 images from Kaggle) with significant class imbalance (1,261 nevus vs. 299 melanoma), limiting generalizability.
No external validation was performed; the model was only tested on the same Kaggle dataset used for training, raising concerns about overfitting and real-world applicability.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) to compare the performance of the different optimization algorithms (LM, SCG, GDM).
The MLP-LM model showed signs of potential overfitting, with training accuracy (87.3%) notably higher than testing accuracy (73.0%) in the best run, and validation/testing errors exceeding 10%.
The system uses hand-crafted Haar wavelet features rather than end-to-end deep learning, which may limit its ability to capture complex, high-level semantic features compared to CNNs.
Preprocessing steps include resizing images from 3520x2160 to 600x600 pixels, which may result in loss of fine-grained diagnostic details critical for melanoma detection.

The paper acknowledges that variability in image quality and lesion appearance poses a challenge for deployment in clinical settings, but does not provide robustness analysis across diverse imaging conditions.
Training times for SCG and GDM are reported as '0:00:00', which is likely an error or indicates negligible time due to small dataset size, making comparison with LM (1:20:38) potentially misleading.
